title_en: Marriage Law in Kitab al-Umm: Ash-Shafi'i's Analysis
title_ar: أحكام النكاح في كتاب الأم: تحليل الشافعي
content_en:
The marriage chapter of Kitab al-Umm contains some of ash-Shafi'i's most argued and carefully reasoned positions in family law. His direct engagements with the Hanafi and Maliki schools on the wali requirement, the conditions of the marriage contract, and related questions reveal the intellectual dynamism of the formative period of Islamic jurisprudence and help explain why the Shafi'i school took the specific positions it did.

Ash-Shafi'i's argument for the wali requirement as a condition of marriage validity is one of the most sustained arguments in Kitab al-Umm. He quotes the hadith 'There is no marriage without a wali' (Ahmad, Abu Dawud) and argues that this hadith is unambiguous: a marriage contracted by a woman without her wali's participation is void. He engages with the Hanafi position — that an adult woman may contract her own marriage based on the principle of contractual capacity — and argues that the prophetic hadith is a specific exception to the general principle of contractual capacity, and specific texts override general principles in Shafi'i methodology.

Ash-Shafi'i's treatment of the ijbar (compulsory marriage) of a virgin by her father is presented with the hadiths showing that the Prophet allowed fathers to contract their virgin daughters' marriages and that a virgin's silence constitutes consent. He explains that the Islamic social framework of guardianship is protective, not exploitative — the wali's role is to ensure the bride's long-term interests are served, since a young woman may lack the experience and information to evaluate a potential husband's character and circumstances fully.

On the mahr, ash-Shafi'i's Kitab al-Umm establishes the Shafi'i position that there is no minimum requirement — any agreed-upon lawful property is a valid mahr. He directly critiques the Hanafi minimum of ten dirhams, asking: where is the prophetic text establishing this specific minimum? In the absence of a text, ash-Shafi'i holds, a jurist cannot impose a minimum that the Prophet did not specify. His famous response to the Hanafi position — 'The evidence of those who set a minimum is no stronger than the evidence of those who set a different minimum; only the one who sets no minimum follows the texts' — reflects his methodology of restricting obligations to what is explicitly established.

The chapter on prohibited degrees covers the Quranic prohibitions with ash-Shafi'i's characteristic attention to language. His analysis of the phrase 'what your right hands possess' (milk al-yamin) in the Quranic marriage verses, and its relationship to the contract of marriage, demonstrates his command of Arabic linguistics in service of legal interpretation.

Ash-Shafi'i's treatment of divorce in Kitab al-Umm covers the forms of talaq, the iddah, and the rights of divorced women. His discussion of the triple talaq counts it as three irrevocable divorces — the position that became standard in the Shafi'i school and is now contested by some contemporary scholars. Ash-Shafi'i presents this position as the understanding of the majority of Companions and Successors, making it a consensus position in his view. His engagement with the hadith of Ibn Abbas (which suggests that triple talaq was counted as one in early Islam before being treated as three) acknowledges the evidence but interprets it as reflecting a particular historical context rather than a permanent legal rule.
title_id: Hukum Pernikahan dalam Kitab al-Umm: Analisis Asy-Syafi'i
content_id:
Bab pernikahan dalam Kitab al-Umm mengandung beberapa posisi asy-Syafi'i yang paling diperdebatkan dan paling hati-hati diargumentasikan dalam hukum keluarga. Keterlibatan langsungnya dengan madzhab Hanafi dan Maliki tentang persyaratan wali, ketentuan-ketentuan mahar, dan syarat-syarat kontrak pernikahan yang sah menjadikan bab ini sumber primer yang diperlukan untuk memahami debat antar-madzhab tentang hukum keluarga dalam fase formatif yurisprudensi Islam klasik.

Argumen asy-Syafi'i untuk persyaratan wali (wali) dalam pernikahan adalah salah satu yang paling rinci dan paling diperdebatkan dalam Kitab al-Umm. Berbeda dengan madzhab Hanafi yang mengizinkan perempuan dewasa menikahkan diri sendiri tanpa wali, asy-Syafi'i berpendapat bahwa perwalian adalah syarat yang tidak dapat dikesampingkan untuk kontrak pernikahan yang sah. Argumennya memanfaatkan beberapa hadis Nabi ﷺ dan responnya terhadap pembacaan Hanafi tentang teks-teks yang sama mencontohkan derajat kejelasan metodologis yurisprudensi asy-Syafi'i.

Bab-bab perceraian dan khulu dalam Kitab al-Umm menunjukkan metodologi asy-Syafi'i yang sama diterapkan pada beberapa pertanyaan hukum keluarga yang paling praktis konsekuensial: kapan perceraian berlaku, apa yang dapat menjadi kondisi-kondisi perceraian yang sah, dan bagaimana hak-hak para pihak berbeda dalam jenis-jenis pemutusan pernikahan yang berbeda. Perlakuan asy-Syafi'i terhadap pertanyaan-pertanyaan ini tetap menjadi acuan bagi pembahasan Syafi'i tentang hukum keluarga di seluruh dunia Muslim.
